text: hello my name is Luis Herrera I am the section head of thoracic surgery at Orlando health in Orlando Florida and I would like to share with you the case of a patient with a t-one be n-one m-zero stage to a non-small cell lung cancer of the left upper lobe on PET scan she had biopsy proven n-one disease in the ten L and eleven L stations the four R and four L stations were negative She presents for a plan for surgical resection, followed by adjuvant therapy.

The surgical team positions robotic XI system ports for a pulmonary lobectomy, with three 8mm ports in the seventh intercostal space and two 12mm ports placed anteriorly and posteriorly for the assistant. Instruments used include a bipolar dissector, cat ear forceps, and a tip-up grasper.

The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to carefully dissect around the pulmonary artery and left lower lobe bronchus, providing a posterior view. The dissection focuses on separating the level of ten L lymph nodes while maintaining a periaventusial plane, minimizing lymph node fragmentation for a clean and bloodless procedure.

The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to perform precise dissection around the pulmonary vessels in the subcranial space from the left side. A finger of a glove is utilized to harvest or pass off nodes when there is suspicion of nodal involvement with metastatic disease.

The surgeon carefully frees up the lymph node near the proximal hilum to avoid fragmentation, focusing on small vessels supplying the node and maintaining a dry field. The dissection progresses proximally along the left main bronchus, with the left main pulmonary artery visible to the left of the carriera instrument. The vagus nerve and aorta are visible on the right side of the screen as the surgeon attempts to completely resect the node due to suspected disease.

The surgeon uses a glove to pass off the removed lymph node, ensuring complete removal. The posterior hilum is explored, and CO₂ insufflation is used to develop the fissure plane, which is well-developed in this patient. In cases with less complete fissures, the fissure is addressed last to minimize air leaks, similar to a VATS approach.

The surgeon harvests level XI L lymph nodes and exposes the pulmonary artery branches. The lingular artery branch is visible on the left side of the screen, while the posterior segment branch is also identified. The surgical field is cleared of lymph nodes to facilitate a complete lymphadenectomy and create space for stapler passage.

The surgeon uses the XI system's control stapler, with two inferior twelve millimeter trocar ports for assistance and left-hand access, allowing selection of an anterior or posterior angle for stapling. The stapler is equipped to carry various loads, including gray, white, blue, and green.

The surgeon continues dissecting the hilar lymph nodes to expose the next artery leading to the apical segment. Careful dissection in the periaventusial plane is performed to fully expose the vessel and avoid collisions with the lymph node when passing the stapler. The lingular artery is then taken, with dissection kept close to the vessel wall to maintain a dry field and achieve a complete lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon uses a caviar forcep through the anterior trocar to position the stapler for optimal engagement with the vessel. The elastic vessel loop is available for retraction but is not used. The surgeon practices the stapler angle before engaging the vessel, ensuring minimal lifting or stretching. The lingular branch is dissected, and the surgeon proceeds to dissect between the superior and inferior pulmonary veins on the left side, creating a window under the parenchyma to complete the anterior aspect of the major fissure.

The surgeon uses a vessel loop to retract the lung parenchyma and employs a 45mm stapler to complete the fissure. Following this, the surgeon meticulously dissects level XI hilar lymph nodes, aiming for a radical lymphadenectomy due to suspected N-I disease. The robotic system facilitates precise dissection with reduced fragmentation of lymph nodes. The excised lymph nodes are placed in a glove and passed off through the assistant trocar.

The surgeon exposes the oropulmonary window with assistance in retracting the hilum, revealing the plane between the superior pulmonary vein and the left main pulmonary artery. Anterior level X lymph nodes are excised, freeing the superior wall of the pulmonary vein from the first branch of the artery. The bifurcation of the branch is visible, with a lymph node obstructing the window. The surgeon opts to take the vein first, utilizing a dual twelve millimeter trocar setup for a posterior angle approach. The robotic arm is repositioned to the assistant's trocar, allowing the stapler to approach from a lower angle, minimizing collisions with the pulmonary artery. The stapler is closed and fired using a wide load, with tension relieved to minimize bleeding from the staple line.

The surgeon examines the pulmonary artery, noting the bifurcation with the left pulmonary artery after the vein has been divided. Due to limited visibility in the tight window, an elastic vessel loop is used to retract the tissue. The lung is flipped forward to mobilize a lymph node and increase the window for the stapler. The surgeon carefully rotates the lung to visualize the stapler tip before advancing, ensuring precise engagement. Finally, the bronchus is dissected.

The surgeon uses a green load stapler from a posterior approach, parallel to the ongoing left lower lobe bronchus. The left upper lobe is elevated and taken at the takeoff, with all hilar nodes already skeletonized and cleaned out. The surgeon then addresses the calcified AP window nodes at level five, ensuring a complete lymphadenectomy due to nodal metastatic disease in the hilum.
The patient went home in two days. Operative time was two hours. Thank you so much for your attention.
